concepts.title: Solving Differential Equation of the First Order but of Higher Degree
concepts.conceptBody:
To solve differential equation of the first order but of higher degree

Standard forms of differential equation of the first order and higher degree are as follows (here we denote $\frac{dy}{dx}$ by p).

  • Solvabale for $\mathbf{p}$. 

Form $\left\{ p-{{f}_{1}}\left( x,y \right) \right\}\left\{ p-{{f}_{2}}\left( x,y \right) \right\}\ldots \left\{ p-{{f}_{n}}\left( x,y \right) \right\}=0$

Method Solve the first order and first degree equation $p-{{f}_{1}}\left( x,y \right)=0$ etc. If $\phi \left( x,y,c \right)=0$, etc. are solutions (taking the same arbitrary constant $\text{c}$ for each) then ${{\phi }_{1}}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{c} \right).{{\phi }_{2}}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{c} \right)\ldots {{\phi }_{\text{n}}}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{c} \right)=0$ is the solution.

  • Solvable for y. Form $y=f\left( x,p \right)$

Method. Differentiate $y=f\left( x,y \right)$ w.r.t. x which gives a first order and first degree equation in $\text{p}$ and $\text{x}$. Solve it to get $\phi \left( \text{x},\text{p},\text{c} \right)=0$.

Eliminating p from $y=f\left( x,p \right)$ and $\phi \left( x,p,c \right)=0$

  • Solveable for x Form $x=f\left( y,p \right)$ 

Method. Differentiate $x=f\left( y,p \right)$ w.r.t. y, which gives a first order and first degree equation in p and y. Solve it to get $\psi \left( \text{y},\text{p},\text{c} \right)=0$. Eliminate $\text{p}$ from $\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{y},\text{p} \right)$ and $\psi \left( \text{y},\text{p},\text{c} \right)=0$.

  • Clairaut's equation. Form $y=px+f\left( p \right)$ 

Method. Differentiate w.r.t. x, we get

$\begin{matrix} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left\{ \text{x}+\text{{f}'}\left( \text{p} \right) \right\}\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}=0 \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\text{p}=\text{c }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {f}'}\left( \text{p} \right)+\text{x}=0 \\ \end{matrix}$

When $\text{p}=\text{c}$, the general solution is $\text{y}=\text{cx}+\text{f}\left( \text{c} \right)$ which gives a family of straight lines.

When ${f}'\left( p \right)+x=0$, eliminating p from $y=px+f\left( p \right)$ and ${f}'\left( p \right)+x=0$ we get a solution which is a curve (without any arbitrary constant) touching all the lines given by $y=cx+f\left( c \right)$. This solution is called the singular solution.

 

Q) Solve $x{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}+\left( y-x \right)\frac{dy}{dx}-y=0$.

Solution : 

Here $x{{p}^{2}}+\left( y-x \right)p-y=0$ where $p=\frac{dy}{dx}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{or } & \text{x}{{\text{p}}^{2}}-\text{xp}+\text{yp}-\text{y}=0 \\ \therefore & \text{xp}(\text{p}-1)+\text{y}(\text{p}-1)=0 \\ \text{or } & (\text{p}-1)(\text{xp}+\text{y})=0 \\ \text{or } & (\text{p}-1)(\text{xp}+\text{y})=0 \\ \therefore & \text{p}-1=0\quad \text{ or }\quad \text{xp}+\text{y}=0 \\ \end{array}$

$\text{Now, p}-1=0\,\,\,\Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=1$

$\Rightarrow \text{y}=\text{x}+\text{c}\,$

$\text{xp}+\text{y}=0=0\Rightarrow \,\,\,\text{x}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}+\text{y}=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{xdy}+\text{ydx}=0$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \text{d}(\text{xy})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \text{xy}=\text{c} \\ \end{align}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ the general solution is $\left( y-x-c \right)\left( xy-c \right)=0$.

 

 

Q) Solve $y=2x\frac{dy}{dx}+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}$ .

Solution : 

Here, $y=2xp+{{p}^{2}}$ where $p=\frac{dy}{dx}$.

It is in the form $y=f\left( x,p \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ [solvabale for $y$ ]

Differentiate w.r.t. $\text{x}$,

$\begin{align} & p=2p+2x\frac{dp}{dx}+2p\frac{dp}{dx} \\ & \therefore \quad 2(\text{x}+\text{p})\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}+\text{p}=0\, \\ & or\quad p\frac{dx}{dp}+2x+2p=0 \\ \end{align}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dp}}+\frac{2}{\text{p}}\text{x}=-2$ ...(i)

The integrating factor

$={{\text{e}}^{\int{\frac{2}{\text{p}}}\text{dp}}}={{\text{e}}^{2\log \text{p}}}={{\text{e}}^{\log {{\text{p}}^{2}}}}={{\text{p}}^{2}}.$

Multiplying (1) by ${{\text{p}}^{3}}$.

$\begin{align} & {{\text{p}}^{2}}\frac{d\text{x}}{\text{dp}}+2\text{px}=-2{{\text{p}}^{2}} \\ & \therefore \,\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dp}}\left( {{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{x} \right)=-2{{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ & \text{or}\,\text{d}\left( {{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{x} \right)=-2{{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{dp} \\ & \therefore \,{{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{x}=\int ~-2{{\text{p}}^{2}}\text{dp}=-\frac{2{{\text{p}}^{3}}}{3}+\frac{\text{c}}{3}.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{align}$

Thus, we have $y=2xp+{{p}^{2}}$ and $3{{p}^{2}}x+2{{p}^{3}}=c$

$\begin{align} & \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2py-c=2p\left( 2xp+{{p}^{2}} \right)-\left( 3{{p}^{2}}x+2{{p}^{3}} \right)~ \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=4x{{p}^{2}}-3{{p}^{2}}x={{x}^{2}}=x\left( y-2xp \right) \\ \end{align}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore & p{{(2y+2x)}^{2}}=xy+c \\ \therefore & p=\frac{xy+c}{2y+2{{x}^{2}}}. \\ \end{array}$

Putting this in $y=2xp+{{p}^{2}}$

$\begin{align} & y=2x\cdot \frac{xy+c}{2y+2{{x}^{2}}}+\left( \frac{xy+c}{2y+2{{x}^{2}}} \right) \\ & \therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,4y{{\left( y+{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{2}}=4x\left( xy+c \right)\left( y+{{x}^{2}} \right)+{{(xy+c)}^{2}} \\ \end{align}$

 

Q) Solve $1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}=x\frac{dy}{dx}$

Solution : 

Here,$xp=1+{{p}^{2}}$ where $p=\frac{dy}{dx}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\frac{1}{\text{p}}+\text{p}$ ...(i)

which is of the form $x=f\left( y,p \right)$ {solvable for $x\}$

Differentiating (1) w.r.t. y

$\begin{align} & \frac{1}{p}=\left( -\frac{1}{{{p}^{2}}}+1 \right)\frac{dp}{dy} \\ & \text{ or }\quad dy=p\left( 1-\frac{1}{{{p}^{2}}} \right)dp \\ & \therefore y+c=\int{\left( p-\frac{1}{p} \right)}dp \\ \end{align}$

$ory+x=\frac{{{p}^{2}}}{2}-\log p$ ...(ii)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ the p-eliminating, obtained by eliminating $\text{p}$ from (1) and (2), is the general solution.

 

Q) Solve $y=x\frac{dy}{dx}+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}$. 

Solution : 

Here, $y=xp+{{p}^{2}}$ where $p=\frac{dy}{dx}$; this is of the form $y=xp+f\left( p \right)$.

Differentiating w.r.t. x

$\text{p}=\text{p}+\text{x}\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}+2\text{p}\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}$

$\therefore (\text{x}+2\text{p})\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}=0;\quad \therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\text{x}+2\text{p}=0\text{ or }\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}=0\text{ }$

$\frac{\text{dp}}{\text{dx}}=0$

$\left. \Rightarrow \text{p}=\text{c} \right]$

$\begin{align} & \therefore \quad \text{y}=\text{xp}+{{\text{p}}^{2}}gives\,\text{y}=\text{cx}+{{\text{c}}^{2}} \\ & x+2p=0\quad \Rightarrow p=-\frac{x}{2} \\ \end{align}$

concepts.title: Some Facts of Cartesian Curve useful in solving problems
concepts.conceptBody:
Cartesian Curve

Facts from cartesian curve: 

  1. Slope of tangent at any point $P\left( x,y \right)=\frac{dy}{dx}$
  2. Equation of tangent $\text{PQ}$ at $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ is $\text{Y}-\text{y}=\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}\left( \text{X}-\text{x} \right)$
  3. Equation of normal PR at $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ is $\text{Y}-\text{y}=-\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dy}}\left( \text{X}-\text{x} \right)$
  4. Length of trangent $\text{PQ}$ at $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)=\text{y}\sqrt{1+{{\left( \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dy}} \right)}^{2}}}$
  5. Length of normal PG at $\left( x,y \right)=y\sqrt{1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}}$
  6. Length of subtangent QM at $\left( x,y \right)=y\cdot \frac{dx}{dy}$
  7. Length of subnormal MR at $\left( x,y \right)=y\cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$.

 

Q) Find the equation of curve whose subtangent is n times the abscissa of the point of contact. 

Solution : 

If (x,y) be any point on the curve, the subtangent at $\left( x,y \right)=y\frac{dx}{dy}$ ...(i)

Now $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dy}}=\text{nx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (given)

or $\text{n}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{y}}=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}$

Integrating, $\text{nlogy}=\text{logx}+\text{logc}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{y}^{\text{n}}}=\text{cx}$ which is the required equation of the family of curves.

 

Q) Find the equation of curve in which the length of the subnornal in twice the square of the ordinate. 

Solution : 

The subnormal at any point (x,y) on the curve is given by $y\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & y\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)=2{{y}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (given) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{dy}{y}=2dx \\ \end{array}$

Integrating, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ logy}=2\text{x}+\text{c}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{y}{c}={{e}^{2x}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=c{{e}^{2x}}$

 

Q) Find the equation of the curve for which the length of the normal at any point is $\text{k}$ times square of the ordinate. 

Solution : 

Given that $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}\sqrt{1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}}=\text{k}{{\text{y}}^{2}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}={{k}^{2}}{{y}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=\sqrt{{{k}^{2}}{{y}^{2}}-1}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}{{y}^{2}}-1}}=dx$ ...(i)

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{\sqrt[x]{{{y}^{2}}-1/{{x}^{2}}}}=dx$ ...(i)

Now solve it using normal integration

 

Q) Find equation of the cure in which the perpendicular from the origin upon the tangent is equal to the abscissa of point of contact. 

Solution : 

Let $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ be any point on the curve equation of tangent at $\text{P}$ is

$\begin{align} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,y-y=\frac{dy}{dx}(x-x) \\ & orX\frac{dy}{dx}-Y+y-x\frac{dy}{dx}=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(\,i) \\ \end{align}$

Now, length of perpendicular from then origin (0,0) on (i) is x

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{0-0+y-x\frac{dy}{dx}}{\sqrt{{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}+1}}=x\quad \Rightarrow \quad {{\left( y-x\frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}}={{x}^{2}}\left( 1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}^{2}} \right)$

$or{{y}^{2}}-2xy\frac{dy}{dx}={{x}^{2}}$

$or\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{{{y}^{2}}-{{x}^{2}}}{2xy}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(\,ii)$

$\text{v}+\text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2\text{vx}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}-1}{2\text{v}}\quad$ [putting $\text{y}=\text{vx}$ in (ii)]

$\Rightarrow \text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}-1}{2\text{v}}-\text{v}=\frac{-1-{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{2\text{v}}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{2\text{v}}{1+{{\text{v}}^{2}}}\text{dv}+\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}=0$

Integrating $\left( 1+{{v}^{2}} \right)x=c$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}=\text{cx}$

 

Q) Find the equation of the curve passing through (2,1) which has constant subtangent. 

Solution : 

The length of subtangent is constant. Using this property, we can define the differential equation of the curve i.e. subtangent $=\frac{y}{{{y}'}}=k$ where $\text{k}$ is a constant.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ k}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\text{y}$

Integrate both sides to get :

$\int{\frac{\text{kdy}}{\text{y}}}=\int{\text{dx}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ klogy}=\text{x}+\text{c}$ where c is an arbitrary constant.

As the required curve passes through (2,1), it lies on it.

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow 0=2+\text{k}\quad \Rightarrow \text{c}=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow the\,equation\,of\,the\,curve\,is:k\log y=x-2. \\ \end{align}$

Note that above equation can also be put in the form $y=A{{e}^{Bx}}.$

 

Q) Find the curve through (2,0) so that the segment of tangent between point of tangency and y-axis has a constant length equal to 2 .

Solution : 

The segment of the tangent between the point of tangency and y-axis has a constant length

$=\text{PT}=2$

Using this property, we can define the differential equation of the curve i.e.

$\text{PT}=\text{x}\sec \theta =\text{x}\sqrt{1+{{\tan }^{2}}\theta }=\text{x}\sqrt{1+{{\text{y}}^{\prime 2}}}\quad $

 

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \text{x}\sqrt{1+{{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}^{2}}}=2\quad \\ & \Rightarrow 1+{{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}^{2}}=\frac{4}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}\quad \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\pm \sqrt{\frac{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}} \\ \end{align}$

Intgerate both sides to get :

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\pm \int ~\sqrt{\frac{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}\text{dx}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}$

Put $\text{x}=2\,\text{sin}\,\theta \Rightarrow \text{dx}=2\,\text{cos}\,\theta \,\text{d}\,\theta $

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \text{y}=2\pm \int{\frac{{{\cos }^{2}}\theta }{\sin \theta }}\text{d}\,\theta +{{\text{c}}_{1}}=\pm \int{(\operatorname{cosec}\theta -\sin \theta )}\,\text{d}\,\theta +{{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\pm (2\log |\operatorname{cosec}\theta -\cot \theta |+2\cos \theta )+\text{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text{y}=\pm 2\log \left( \left| \frac{2-\sqrt{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}{\text{x}} \right|+\sqrt{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right)+\text{c} \\ \end{align}$

As (2,0) lies on the curve, it should satisfy its equation, i.e. $c=0$.

$\Rightarrow the\,equation\,of\,the\,curve\,is:\text{y}=\pm 2\log \left( \left| \frac{2-\sqrt{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}{\text{x}} \right|+\sqrt{4-{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right)$

concepts.title: Initial Value Problem done for First Order First Degree Differential Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Initial Value Problem

In initial value problem along with a differential equation some initial values are given (i.e., corresponding values of x and y). Using the initial values, we determine the value of constant c to get a particular solution of given differential equation.

 

Q) Solve the following initial value problems: 

(i) $\frac{dy}{dx}=2{{e}^{x}}{{y}^{3}},y\left( 0 \right)=\frac{1}{2}$ (ii) ${{\text{e}}^{\text{dy}/\text{dx}}}=\text{x}+1,\text{y}\left( 0 \right)=3$ 
(iii) $\text{xy}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\text{y}+2,\text{y}\left( 2 \right)=0$ (iv) $\left( 1+{{\text{y}}^{2}} \right)\left( 1+\text{logx} \right)\text{dx}+\text{xdy}=0,\text{y}\left( 1 \right)=1$

Solution: 

(i) Given differential equation is

$\frac{dy}{dx}=2{{e}^{x}}{{y}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{{{y}^{3}}}=2{{e}^{x}}dx$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int{\frac{dy}{{{y}^{3}}}}=2\int{{{e}^{x}}}dx\quad \Rightarrow -\frac{1}{2{{y}^{2}}}=2{{e}^{x}}+c$ ...(i)

$Now,\,\,y\,(0)=\frac{1}{2}\quad \Rightarrow when\,\,x=0,y=\frac{1}{2}$

Thus $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\frac{1}{2{{\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)}^{2}}}=2{{\text{e}}^{0}}+\text{c}$ From (i)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-2=2+c\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow c=-4$

$\therefore $ From (i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\frac{1}{2{{y}^{2}}}=2{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}-4$

which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

(ii) Given differential equation is

$\begin{align} & {{\text{e}}^{\text{dy}/\text{dx}}}=\text{x}+1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}}=\log (\text{x}+1)\quad \Rightarrow \text{dy}=\log (\text{x}+1)\text{d}x \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\begin{align} & \,\,\,\,\int{1}~\text{d}y=\int{\log }(\text{x}+1)\text{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text{y}\,\,\,=\int{\log }(\text{x}+1)\cdot 1\text{dx} \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\log (\text{x}+1)\cdot \text{x}-\int{\frac{\text{x}}{\text{x}+1}}\text{dx}\,\, \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\text{x}\log (\text{x}+1)-\int{\left[ 1-\frac{1}{\text{x}+1} \right]}\text{dx}\,\, \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\text{x}\log (\text{x}+1)-\int{1}\text{dx}+\int{\frac{1}{\text{x}+1}}\text{dx}\, \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{y}=\text{x}\log (\text{x}+1)-\text{x}+\log |\text{x}+1|+\text{c }...\text{(i)} \\ \end{align}$
$\begin{align} & Now,\,\text{y}(0)=3\Rightarrow \,\,when\,\text{x}=0,\text{y}=3\,\, \\ & Thus,3=0-0+\log 1+\text{c}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[From(i)] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text{c}=3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[\because \log 1=0] \\ & \therefore From\,\,(i),\,\,y=x\log (x+1)-x+\log |x+1|+3 \\ \end{align}$

which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

(iii) Given differential equation is

\(xy\frac{dy}{dx}=y+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \frac{y}{y+2}dy=\frac{dx}{x}\)

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int ~\frac{y}{y+2}dy=\int ~\frac{dx}{x}\Rightarrow \int ~\left[ 1-\frac{2}{y+2} \right]dy=\int ~\frac{dx}{x}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int 1\text{dy}-2\int \frac{1}{\text{y}+2}\text{dy}=\int \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}-2\text{log}\left| \text{y}+2 \right|=\text{log}\left| \text{x} \right|+\text{c}$

Now, $y\left( 2 \right)=0\Rightarrow $ when $\text{x}=2,\text{y}=0$

Thus, $0-2\text{log}2=\text{log}2+\text{c}$ [From (i)]

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }c=-3\text{log}2$

$\therefore $ from (i), $y-2\text{log}\left| y+2 \right|=\text{log}\left| x \right|-3\text{log}2$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ log}{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}-\text{log}{{(\text{y}+2)}^{2}}=\text{logx}-\text{log}8$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ log}\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}}{{{(\text{y}+2)}^{2}}}=\text{log}\frac{\pi }{8}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}}{{{(\text{y}+2)}^{2}}}=\frac{\text{x}}{8}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }8{{\text{e}}^{\text{y}}}=\text{x}{{(\text{y}+2)}^{2}}$

which is the required particular solution of given differential equation.

(iv) Given differential equation is

$\left( 1+{{y}^{2}} \right)\left( 1+\text{log}x \right)dx+xdy=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }xdy=-\left( 1+{{y}^{2}} \right)\left( 1+\text{log}x \right)dx$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{1+{{y}^{2}}}=-\left( \frac{1+\text{log}x}{x} \right)dx$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int{\frac{\text{dy}}{1+{{\text{y}}^{2}}}}=-\int{\frac{1+\log \text{x}}{\text{x}}}\text{dx}$

$\Rightarrow \quad {{\tan }^{-1}}y=-\frac{{{(1+\log x)}^{2}}}{2}+c$ ...(i)

[by putting $1+\text{logx}=\text{t}$]

Now, $y\left( 1 \right)=1\Rightarrow $ when $x=1,y=1$

Thus $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( 1 \right)=-\frac{1}{2}+\text{c}$ [From (i)]

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\text{c }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ From }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\left( \text{i} \right),\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{y}+\frac{{{(1+\text{logx})}^{2}}}{2}=\frac{\pi }{4}+\frac{1}{2}$

which is the required particular solution of the given differential equation.

The equation of a curve passing through $\left(2, \frac{7}{2}\right)$ and having gradient $1-\frac{1}{x^2}$ at $(x, y)$ is
